title: Dysoxylum roseum
taxonomy: Dysoxylum roseum is a species of flowering plant in the family Meliaceae. It is classified within the genus Dysoxylum, which belongs to the order Sapindales. The plant is native to the tropical regions of Asia, including countries such as India, Sri Lanka, and the Philippines. Within the genus Dysoxylum, Dysoxylum roseum is typically grouped together with other species that are similar in appearance and characteristics. Further classification may be made using more specific taxonomic keys, such as the subfamily Cedreloideae.
uses: Dysoxylum roseum is used in traditional medicine to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions. The plant's bark and leaves are believed to possess anti-inflammatory and antiseptic properties, which are utilized to reduce swelling and promote wound healing. In some cultures, Dysoxylum roseum is used to treat digestive issues, such as diarrhea and ulcers, due to its purported ability to soothe the stomach and intestines. Additionally, the plant is sometimes used to treat respiratory problems, such as bronchitis and coughs, due to its supposed expectorant properties. Further research is needed to fully understand the medicinal uses and potential benefits of Dysoxylum roseum.

intro: Dysoxylum roseum, also known as the Philippine rosewood or rose mahogany, is a plant species native to the tropical forests of the Philippines. The plant is valued for its medicinal properties, which include anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant effects, making it beneficial for treating various health conditions such as fever, cough, and skin infections. The bioactive constituents of Dysoxylum roseum include flavonoids, terpenoids, and alkaloids, which contribute to its therapeutic properties. In traditional medicine, the plant is often used to prepare herbal teas, infusions, and extracts, as well as in the form of capsules and tinctures, to take advantage of its health benefits.
